step_by_step_thinking:
Tool Call: retrieve("Tetralogy of Fallot squatting position benefits")

Retrieved Documents: 32 documents

I reviewed the provided references again, specifically looking for mentions of the squatting position and its effects on hemodynamics in patients with Tetralogy of Fallot. References consistently highlight how the squatting position increases venous return, enhancing blood flow to vital organs and particularly aiding in increasing systemic pressure, thus mitigating cyanosis caused by reduced pulmonary blood flow. While reference [14], which discusses perioperative management of congenital heart disease cases involving Tetralogy of Fallot, explicitly recommends the jackknife/ squatting position along with other maneuvers during hypoxic/hypocyanotic spells ("Management... Includes: ...Jack-knife position"), none of the others focus extensively on detailed mechanisms explaining why this maneuver works so effectively.
Given my agreement with the original assertion about squatting benefiting children with Tetralogy of Fallot because it improves systemic circulation and reduces hypoxemic symptoms, I do not find compelling reasons to change my stance even though some sources did not delve deeply into biomechanical details supporting this action. My confidence remains strong enough since established protocols recommend this method widely among experts trained in treating these patients.
Therefore, sticking with our prior consensus,
